item.title: BTS of Matilda
item.description: Fun fact: Danny DeVito, who produced and directed Matilda, and his wife hosted the actress who played Matilda at their house for much of the movie. Her mother was diagnosed with cancer in the middle of filming, and Mara states that having the film to distract her and all of the cast and crew who treated her like family, really helped her with her mother's sickness. Her mom passed away a few months before the movie premiered, but Danny DeVito took an almost completed copy to her hospital and watched it with her shortly before she passed. DeVito and his wife Rhea Pearlman were like uncle and aunt to her.
item.dc:creator: ImpatientMoth

item.title: Antique 1911 Crank Cash Register Restoration - Update #2!
item.description:
(Part 1: https://imgur.com/gallery/3e4uTnd) Alright, so! It's been a week since my first post about this register and I've made some significant progress with the project, as well as uncovered some unexpected challenges. All par for the course, though! As always, if you'd like to materially support this and future restorations you're welcome to drop a buck or two into the fund. ^_^ https://ko-fi.com/elbowdeepinahorse/ With that out of the way, onto the restoration!
Last post, the register looked a lot like this. Intact, caked in barbershop hair and cigarette oil, but by all appearances fully functioning with only a few moderate defects that I knew I'd have to tackle. As with all of these projects, the first step is to take off the outer casing. With this register, a 400 Class according to National Cash Register, that's getting into unfamiliar territory. I've never worked on one of these models before and the way the case is assembled isn't nearly as straightforward as the ones I'm used to.
I started with setting the drawer aside and removing the already unfastened faceplate. This lifted off easily, and to no one's surprise it also revealed a dozen or so more screws I'd be needing to remove before this project was fully underway.
Next comes the crank. I can't take off this side panel with it on the machine, so off it goes. Note the absolute caking of grime on the flat panel below it. This register may not have gotten stowed away in a barn or in the weather, but it was still able to collect an unhealthy layer of dirt and saw very little (if any) cleaning in the 60 years it was used at the barbershop I bought it from. That's not a dig at the owner, either. When I bought my first register I was afraid to touch anything for fear of doing irreparable damage. Unfortunately, the Antiques Roadshow mindset of "never ever touch the patina" is alive and well.
With the crank off, I reattached the lug bolt so I wouldn't lose it.
Next comes the bit of hardware that had come loose from the register drawer.
Easy peasy. I was able to roll it right out. Unfortunately I didn't find any missing hardware, so I'm going to have to find replacements and drill it back on later. Plenty of you suggested I fill the stripped out screw holes in the drawer with wood glue and toothpicks, so I'll be trying that out once I reach that step.
Back to the case, I can't get the side panels off without removing this cap first. Basically, it's just a process of figuring out which sections of the casing attach to neighboring sections and which ones attach directly to the register core. It's a little bit of logic and a little bit of educated guesswork. Since I'm not familiar with this register and I'm coming across a variety of fasteners, I have a whole mess of photos not included in this post that match each panel with their fasteners so I don't mix them up during the rebuild.
I'll set this aside somewhere safe so the glass doesn't get damaged.
Next to come off is the original marble shelf. I put a white box of screws above it to give you a better idea of how yellowed the stone has gotten. I've set it aside for now, but once I start looking at reassembly I'll have to pause long enough to gently wet sand the marble to remove some of the staining. I don't expect all of it to come out, nor will it magically turn white, but it would be nice to get it clean and smooth out the scratches.
This is the first time I've found a marble shelf that isn't screwed into place, but just floats atop two bolt heads to keep it in place. There are matching recesses in the bottom of the shelf to match these, and the front panel keeps the back side of the shelf secure as well. As long as the register doesn't tip over I suppose it's not going anywhere!
The right side panel came off with just three screws, which was a surprise. I suppose there's enough hardware protruding from it to negate the need for more than a few screws along the bottom. Still, makes me nervous!
What I didn't realize, and which nearly fell off on its own when I pulled off that panel, was there is a counter screwed onto the reverse side! this tallies the number of times the drawer has opened, which is a common feature with NCR. Each digit is visible through the peepholes on the top of the panel, something I wasn't aware of until now! Seems I have only a couple thousand more cranks left before it zeroes out.
The windows around the counters come off next and are set aside where the glass will be safe. Hopefully once everything is cleaned up it'll be easier to read the tally behind each!
I know a lot of you like seeing exposed gears, so here's a register nyood for you weirdos. ;) It's all sprocket power from here on out! Now that the topper, the faceplate, and the right panel are off, it's time to look at how to remove the panel behind the receipt printer...
First off is the easy bit. Just a small piece of decor that rounds out the bottom of the printer box.
I was worried at first that I would need to remove the printer mechanism to free the panel, but then I spotted a seam at the top and the screw that held it together. Lo and behold, it's not one solid panel but two halves that meet behind the printer! Say what you will, but that's freaking clever. No need to touch the printer at all! Something worth noting is all the dark discoloration on the brass. My best assumption, being that the register sat in a barbershop since the 1960s, is that something was spilled on the metal that didn't get wiped up. Maybe hair product, maybe just a can of beer, who knows? Whatever it is, it didn't eat away the brass but it certainly created a hot spot for oxidation to start the process. Not the end of the world, but something I know I'll have to contend with while cleaning and polishing.
Here you can see both halves of the left panel, as well as the distinction between the metal that was exposed to the barbershop and the metal that was covered by the printer box. All that splotchy, almost burnt gold coloration along the top and edges is nicotine tar from decades of smoking. Extremely common with just about any antique this old, but it's not going to be any fun to remove. Blech.
With all the panels off, the next step is to pull the register's core off the drawer box and set it aside. This required me to wrestle a socket wrench into the drawer and blindly locate the heads of four bolts while simultaneously keeping a wrench on the square nuts at each bottom corner. It wasn't easy, and I know it's going to be a stupendous pain in the ass to put them back in when I'm done. >_
Bam! Now it's just down to the drawer box. I have a bunch of photos documenting the step by step disassembly, mainly so I can keep track of all the crossbars you can sort of see beneath the open panels. Suffice to say, it wasn't a complicated disassembly so I won't post all those here. The top came off, then the sides. Bingo bango bongo. ;)
Something I noticed well before starting this project was the abundance of missing fasteners on the bottom of the drawer. That, more than anything else, was my chief concern. There are meant to be heavy duty bolts securing the wooden base and its components to the sidewalls, and there are more missing than present. My assumption is that whoever damaged the wooden base tried - somehow - to remove it from the rest of the machine and realized they were making an awful mistake before they finished. I'm glad they did if that's the case, because there would have been a good chance the weight of all that metal on three unsecured sidewalls would have folded them like a lawn chair and probably injured their would-be technician in the process.
Another missing bolt on the opposite corner. Also, lots of hair. So much hair.
Ta-da! It's just that easy! (sobs in sore screwdriving wrist)
That center bar is meant to be the track that guides the drawer. It's also missing a bolt on the far end, adding yet another specific fastener to my lengthening shopping list. Thanks, previous owner! It's worth mentioning that it took me the better part of 3 hours to get to this point.
My guess is that someone tried to slide the register and, lacking the corner fasteners, the weight of the machine provided enough resistance for the wood glue to simply give way at the joint. Thankfully it didn't break off the notch (dovetail?) and should be relatively easy to clean up and reglue.
Bonus points for this wiggly boi staying intact. It's structural support days are probably over, but I'm going to leave it in place while I get gluey.
Thankfully everything came back together after I pressed the edge of that steel baseplate back into its groove. I brushed out the gaps, gave it a bit of rasping with steel wire, gave each corner a generous helping of wood glue, and rubber malleted the gaps shut. I don't own clamps long enough to grip the frame, so there's a small gap yet along the seam. I'm not super worried about it, personally. What I'll likely do before the project is done is collect some sawdust from the bottom side, mix it into some glue, and use it to patch up the gaps.
With that done, it's back to the register core and all the little metal backers I left between the buttons.
These things are FILTHY. The keys are gross, the brass backplates are awful, and every gap is packed with hairy gunk. Blech. Blech blech blech. Also, a few of the glass windows for the keys pulled away from the painted wood slugs, giving them a hazy appearance. I'm on the fence on whether to leave them be or risk damage by prying up the metal rings that hold them in place.
Crunchy.
I was curious more than anything else about the state of the paint beneath the grime. Some rubbing alcohol on a cotton swab gave me the answer. The paint is in great condition!
About 2 minutes of careful cleaning brought the red no-sale bar back to life without any chipping! I'm not going to attempt doing this to the number wheels, however, since they're already in excellent condition.
So much finger grime. Aughckhgkc.
I used a boatload of rubbing alcohol, cotton swabs, and a fine dental pick to remove the gunk. The silver rings around each key are a future Ellie problem, because cleaning those is going to suck.
As is tradition, all the dirty panels get dragged downstairs.
Anything that's still screwed on needs to get removed by this point, including levers and handles. The last thing I want to do is find a dried pocket of soap or Brasso tucked away after it's all put back together. As far as cash registers go, this one had around double the brass compared to the other ones I've worked on. Cleaning these off and polishing them took me two nights of work, yesterday and today. I had to take a break before my back and shoulder forced me to stop. I can only spend so many hours stooping over a utility sink and scrubbing. @.@
I tend not to take many photos while I'm cleaning these because it's such an absolute mess of a process, so I tried to do you guys a bit better this time. The first step in cleaning the panels is to remove as much of the nicotine tar, dirt, and dust as possible. It doesn't pay to waste brass cleaner on metal that's still insulated with grime. This time around I'm trying out a more budget approach by ditching the Dawn Platinum degreaser I usually use with some generic degreasing dish soap.
Overall... eh, results aren't great. It loosened up some of the tar, but you can see there's a lot of it hanging on. The dish soap alternative was worth a try but after a few more rounds of scrubbing I eventually called it quits and started using the expensive stuff. What I'd really like to do is find some junk brass and experiment with different products, possibly oven cleaner, to see which ones damage the metal and which don't. I've learned not to put too much stock in what the label says when it comes to metal cleaner. Ultimately it comes down to trial and error.
This panel alone took me about 15 minutes to finish. After I pulled as much grime off of it as I could, I slathered it with Brasso and scrubbed it in with a junk rag. The results are similar if not the same as what I was getting using a buffing wheel, which tells me I was wasting my own time on that step. The buffing wheel is liable to be handy for some applications, but the time I was taking to scrub out all the leftover polishing compound just isn't worth it for these big panels. It's a good tool to have, but it isn't a swiss army knife. I spent the remainder of Saturday getting through about half of the metal I needed to before I had to call it. I am a sore horse lady.
I picked things up today and powered through the rest of the brass, ending on this foul piece of metal which took a full hour of my time and the last of my muscles. This is the panel that the register core and marble shelf sat on for the last 113 years, and the metal beneath them is more or less untouched save for the liquids that seeped beneath them. The left and right sides, however, were an absolute casserole of caked up grime. I knew that was something I'd need to expect given they're the natural place people would set their hands, or put stuff on, but holy crap this thing kicked my ass.
This is what it looked like *after* I degreased and scrubbed the hell out of it with Brasso. That black junk would not give up. I brought it upstairs where I keep my rotary tool, thinking I would just blast the crud off with a stiff bristle brush, but all I managed to do was make the gunk shiny.
It's like Hexus from Ferngully dragged his toxic ballsack over the metal, augh.
We hates it! We hates its filthses on the precious!!
Ultimately, the solution came down to dragging it back downstairs and attacking the residue with a combination of Brasso and a steel bristle brush. That was an uncomfortable endeavor for me since steel is harder than brass, and I was constantly afraid that I would look down to see brass plating flaking off the base metal. I was careful. I stopped regularly to check my progress, rinsed off the excess Brasso mud I was creating to see where I should be focusing, and with the help of my dental picks I was able to pull up almost all of that grime without damaging the underlying metal.
At the end of the day, I consider it to be a huge win. And I don't ever want to have to resort to steel on brass ever again. @.@"
Here's the end result so far! Pardon my dungeon of a basement. It ain't pretty but it's pretty damn good for dirty work! :3
Obligatory wide shot. My arms are killing me, my back is nearly ready to mutiny, and I've got a blister the size of a quarter on the heel of my left hand, but after three nights of work the biggest hurdle of this project is behind me! Next steps include: - Sourcing fasteners to replace all that are missing (RIP my wallet) - Replacing the broken spring on the back of the cash drawer (can you find the donor drawers in this photo?) - Cleaning the nickel on each button - Repairing the holes at the back of the drawer so I can reinstall the track guide - Determine whether the wooden base needs to be cleaned, or should be resanded, stained, and sealed. - Clean all the fasteners - Clean all the glass - Rebuild the register - ??? - Profit!
In the meantime, I'm going to sit here and decide how many ibuprofen I can safely take before my liver implodes. Also that's a joke, I'm just taking two, please don't freak out. I'm freaking SORE. D: Thanks again to everyone who enjoys these updates! Depending on how my body recovers I'll either have another progress report next weekend, or the one after that. If you're really desperate to know how it's going in the meantime, there's a chance you might catch a thing or two on my Discord (https://discord.gg/imgurstable) or on my Kofi page (https://ko-fi.com/elbowdeepinahorse/). Big chunky updates though will happen here on Imgur, because you guys are awesome and I can't get enough of sharing this part of my life with you weirdos even if it isn't horse memes. ;3 Anyhoo, that's it for tonight! I'm going to Culver's for dinner, aaaa!
item.dc:creator: ElbowDeepInAHorse

item.title: Monday Morning Music Dump #2
item.description:
Morning music lovers. Let’s get this bread
We are just getting started!
Big hair, don’t care
Recommendation from a friend
So fluffy
Take a deeo one and get out there!
Constantinople
Killing it
No theme music???
So many steps
Goats!
Almost there
Get bussin
Welcome to the chill zone
Like a boss
Heavy sh*t
Dog tax. Thanks for stopping by! Edit: Most Viral! Send some tunes or something nice. Thanks!
item.dc:creator: cobblersays

item.title: good lord
item.description: Some of you may remember that she and Grohl had a drum off and she was invited to play Everlong on one of their concerts. Both songs are extremely tough on drums and performed by Dave Grohl on the albums. My favourite drum cover, also Dave Grohl, also off the same QOTSA album: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dxwkjQ_Y5Rs
item.dc:creator: majortool

Pg. 2
item.dc:creator: LincLoud

item.title: Once in a lifetime !
item.description:
Yesterday I had the great honor to be a torche bearer for the Paris Olympics Games 2024 ! The Olympic flame travels all around France, to finish in Paris !
There are 11.000 torch bearer and I was one of them, in the city of Nogaro ! I am not a professional sportwoman, but I ran 1500km (about 950 miles) in 3 years with my dog Cookie ! And I had a send a motivation letter to be candidate !
Every torch bearer has to walk the Olympic flame for 200 meters (220 yards)
And this is a gift we all received at the end : an engraved part of the torch ! (It's the middle part)
Cookie and I on a canicross run !
item.dc:creator: marielaure

item.title: when you mess up so bad that mom revokes your right to breath
